title: Poule au pot
subtitle: Whole chicken poached in broth with vegetables
excerpt: A whole chicken poached gently for an hour and a half in a pot of water with carrots, leeks, celery, and aromatics until the broth is rich and the bird falls from the bone. The broth is served first; the chicken and vegetables follow.
sourceNotes: Poule au pot is attributed to Henri IV of France, who in 1600 expressed the wish that every peasant family should have 'une poule au pot' every Sunday. The story is likely apocryphal, but the dish itself is documented as a French country classic from at least the seventeenth century. It is the direct ancestor of the English boiling fowl tradition and the Italian bollito misto.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The broth can be made a day ahead and refrigerated; skim the solidified fat from the top before reheating.

content.text: Poule au pot is one of the simplest things you can make, and one of the most satisfying: a whole chicken, submerged in cold water, brought slowly to a gentle simmer, and coaxed for ninety minutes until the cooking liquid has become a proper broth and the chicken is tender throughout. You get two courses from one pot: the broth first, with a few of the smaller vegetables, and the chicken and remaining vegetables after.

The key is temperature. The pot must never boil: a steady, barely visible simmer keeps the broth clear and the chicken moist. A rolling boil turns the broth grey and the breast meat stringy. Skim the surface foam in the first fifteen minutes and you will end up with a clear, golden broth worth drinking on its own.

Place the {{chicken-whole}} in a large casserole or pot. Add the {{onion}}, {{garlic}}, {{thyme}}, {{bay-leaves}}, {{peppercorns-black}}, and a generous pinch of salt. Cover with cold water by at least 5 cm. Bring to a very gentle simmer over medium heat — this takes 20 minutes. Skim the grey foam from the surface diligently for the first 15 minutes until no more rises. Reduce to the lowest simmer and poach for 30 minutes.

Add the {{carrot}}, {{leek}}, {{celery}}, and {{turnip}} to the pot. Continue poaching at a bare simmer for a further 50 to 60 minutes. The chicken is done when a skewer inserted into the thigh joint runs clear, or when the drumstick pulls away from the body with gentle pressure. Let the chicken rest in the broth for 10 minutes before lifting out.

Ladle the broth into bowls with a few pieces of the softer vegetables and scatter {{parsley-flat}} over. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve the broth first. Then lift the chicken onto a board, carve or pull the meat, arrange on a platter with the remaining vegetables, and serve with Dijon mustard, coarse sea salt, and more parsley.

items.cause: Pot boiled rather than simmered; foam not skimmed
items.fix: Start in cold water; skim diligently in the first 15 minutes; maintain the lowest possible simmer — the surface should barely quiver

items.symptom: Breast meat is dry and stringy
items.cause: Boiled at too high a temperature
items.fix: The breast needs gentle, consistent heat; if it dried out, slice it thin and serve with extra broth spooned over

items.cause: Not enough salt, or too much water for the pot size
items.fix: Season generously from the start; the ratio of chicken to water matters — the pot should be just large enough to hold the chicken

items.cause: Added too early and cooked for the full 90 minutes
items.fix: Add vegetables at the halfway point (30 minutes in); they need only 50 to 60 minutes of gentle heat

content.text: Poule au pot is the French equivalent of a Sunday roast without the roast: a whole bird cooked in water, producing both a meal and a broth in a single vessel. The Henri IV attribution (he supposedly promised a chicken in every peasant's pot every Sunday) gave the dish a political resonance it otherwise wouldn't have earned. In practice it was the dish of farmhouses and modest households, a way of making a tough older bird (a poule rather than a poulet — a laying hen rather than a roasting chicken) into something good by cooking it long and slow in liquid. Modern versions use younger birds and shorter cooking times. Serve with Dijon mustard and coarse salt in small dishes on the table.
